summary: My father is originally from Michoacán, Mexico, and every December we would return there to celebrate my grandparents' corn crop harvest. It was a special time when I could connect deeply with my father's roots, playing with my cousins and enjoying the foods of his youth. For that one month each year, I truly felt Mexican, immersed in the land and culture of Michoacán.

Mexico is the country where my father's family comes from, specifically Michoacán. Although I was raised in the United States, we traveled back to Mexico annually to reconnect with our heritage and family traditions. Mexico represents the foundation of my father's identity and the place where I felt a strong sense of belonging during those visits.

California is where I was raised and where my American mother is from. My father worked in agriculture there, starting as a field laborer and eventually becoming a field supervisor. Despite the challenges we faced as a Mexican-American family in California, including racial profiling and struggles for rights, it is the place where our family life unfolded and where I grew up.

Jesus is central to my family's faith, especially after the tragic loss of my eldest brother in 2012. We believe that Jehovah has not given us more than He asked of His Son Jesus Christ, and this faith has helped us endure hardship. Jesus' sacrifice inspires us to keep going despite the pain and to trust in God's love and mercy.

Jehovah is the name we use for God, and our family leans on Him especially during difficult times. After my brother was killed in 2012, our faith in Jehovah strengthened as we sought comfort and hope. We believe Jehovah does not burden us beyond what He asked of His Son Jesus Christ, and this belief sustains us through our struggles as Mexican-Americans.

I remember a quote from Seneca the Younger that resonates deeply with my family's experience: 'I am not born for one corner; the whole world is my native land.' This idea reflects our identity as Mexican-Americans who refuse to be confined or marginalized. Despite the obstacles we've faced, we embrace a broader sense of belonging and resilience inspired by Seneca's words.

Jeremiah 29:11 is a verse my family holds onto tightly: 'For I know the plans I have for you... plans to prosper you and not to harm you, plans to give you hope and a future.' This scripture has given us hope through the darkest times, especially after the loss of my brother and the struggles we've endured as immigrants. It reminds us that God has a plan for us beyond our pain.

John 15:13, which says 'No one has greater love than a person who lays down his life for his friends,' became very meaningful to my family after my eldest brother was tragically killed in 2012. This verse helped us understand the depth of sacrifice and love, giving us strength to carry on. It is a cornerstone of our faith and how we interpret the hardships we've faced.

John 10:10, which warns that the devil comes to 'steal, kill, and destroy,' is a passage my family often reflects on. We see the challenges and tragedies we've faced as part of this struggle, but we refuse to let darkness win. Instead, we praise the Lord for His love and mercy, standing firm in our faith despite adversity.
